speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number seven, November 26, 1996, interview with Sonia Bielski. You were telling me about your attitude towards decisions after the war. Did you make some of the decisions on the basis of your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I decided when I will have the children, yes, the only thing what I will sure will give it to them-- will try to give it to them is education. Because without education, they're nothing. And with the education, the whole world is for them. No matter where they will go, they have the education with them. Money's nothing. We saw people what they had a lot of money. They left the money. And they don't know where the money is. And they lost themselves. Money is nothing. In this moment, when you need it, it's very convenience money. But in life, is not everything, money. And my husband was with me always, all the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present attitude towards religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: The what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the same [? Jew. ?] I know that I'm a Jew. I know that I'm a Jew. Am I orthodox, not orthodox, I believe that we have to have tradition. We have, because the tradition is holding our-- our Jewishness. But I, I don't believe in me, I should be orthodox, and make the peyots, and with the other things-- no. No. I think all the people, no matter where they are, or what they are, they are fine and good. Their religion doesn't make them. I know we have many Jew, with the very religion, and they such a bad bastard. Terrible. And I know many Catholic, what they were very good in the times of the war. They took us. They gave it to us food. They put us-- even in the potatoes. They were Polish people. I don't think a Jew would do that-- I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, a Jew, is if he has something from that, he will do it. If he doesn't have nothing from that, he will not do it. That's mine opinion. Maybe I'm wrong. Maybe I'm wrong. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been to Poland since the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was when we came to Warsaw. And I went to visit Majdanek with my husband, and Tuvia, and Lily. Yes, this is the end of that, Poland was. Many people of our friend, they went to Poland. They're saying, Poland, Poland, Poland, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know what they see in Poland. What they can expect from Poland? I know only war. The people what they working here, the women, where they coming, from Poland, they're working, and we are giving them everything. We are putting-- a girl by me was working 15 years. I gave her so, so much. She send everything away to Poland. Yes? And when we came to say goodbye to her, by the plane, I said, OK, Helen, maybe you'll come again? She said, no way. I was working enough for Jewish people. That's enough. I didn't mean Sonia, you. But no more I was want-- wanted to work for Jewish people. Do you frequently think back to the times that you were in the woods?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you sometimes think about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: About Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: About when you were in the forest? In the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- I have near my bed, I put my three brothers, which I lost. And my boys were very mad that I put them near my bed. When my mother and father were alive, the picture was near their bed. Yeah? And when I went away, from Israel, I took this picture with me. Because after me, I don't think the children of mine will remember. They will remember [INAUDIBLE] that they lost three brothers of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want them to know. And I'm asking always, do you remember who they are? They're laughing, sure we know who they are. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my children are going to Israel, they come in-- the first thing they're going to the grave, to my parents. My parents are in kibbutz Dan. And the first thing my-- my baby, when he's coming to kibbutz, before he's going to say hello, to my sister-in-law and brother, he's going to the parents. He said like that to me, Mommy, when I'm coming to the grave for Saba. He-- I know that he hears me. He understand me very well. I don't think Savta understands me. Because two years already, she didn't know nothing. But Saba understood everything. He knew everything. He knew what is the dollar, and what is the-- the franc, and everything. But Saba can hear-- I-- I like to talk to him when I'm coming there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the occupation of your children now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jay is a Ph.D., doctor psychologist, working in the hospital for children, four years old children, without heads. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He's very good. He's the-- the manager of the whole [NON-ENGLISH]. He's very intelligent, very smart, he's a very good boy. David, has two-- he finish-- he finish-- he finish-- he has two masters, my middle one. Two masters. He has masters in-- in political science, and he has another master, but he's not working, not with this, and not with this. He was working, and he was made a lot of money. But when they broke up, that's a year and a half-- he's broken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who broke up?

speaker: interviewee
text: He with his wife. They divorced-- didn't finish the divorce. They have two beautiful daughters. And he's still not-- he's still not working. He couldn't come to grip with this. He loved her very much. And she loved him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she's a very, very rich girl. She's from West Orange. All Oranges, hers buildings. Yes? She didn't have nothing in the house. But her parents live like from me, like from here, here. They were sleeping with them, and eating with them, everything. And he couldn't take it no more. Your youngest son, how old is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My youngest son is 43, never was married. He has too good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He has too good. But now he's ready to marry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is he doing now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's too in the medallions. All of them-- my husband gave it to him the cream for the life. My husband was not working over 20 years. He was semi-retired. He gave to the children to take the fruits of his work. And we was traveling around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is running it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby. He, because everything is now, when my husband died, everything is mine. They know that when I will go away, they know that this is separated, and this is for everybody has the same choice, the same thing. It's divided between three of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have six grandchildren, beautiful grandchildren. I have a bar mitzvah from the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like-- Would you like to tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first is Danielle and Matthew. Danielle is after my mommy. Danielle Pearl. Danielle Pesche. And the boy is Matthew-- Mishulem. The second is three years younger. It's two, a boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they twins?

speaker: interviewee
text: Twins, two pair of twins. She was one in 2 millions, in the times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the second pair of twins is?

speaker: interviewee
text: First is after Zusia's mother, Bella. And the boy is after Margot's grandpa-- grandpa. This is the-- Jay's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilan. This is Jay's four kids. Now, David has two girls, beautiful. One is Rachel Rose. And other one is Jessica-- beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They should never forget what happened to us. And if they will remember, and they will never forget, they will get better for them. Because if you are forgetful, you don't know what happened, what will happen to you. But if you remember, that you had a mother, and father, and grandparents, and where are the grandparents, all of them, where is the family is ours?

speaker: interviewer
text: The German killed them. For what? Because they were Jew? If you remember that, you need a Israel. And because of Israel, we are alive here.

speaker: interviewee
text: If we not have a Israel, we will never-- never be alive here, too. Because they hate us. That's my opinion. And the saying right, we will never forget, and never forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandpa, and grandma, and my mother, and me, and a cousin from Paris. This was probably taken in-- in '37, when they came to visit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandparents names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandpa was Hirsh Nochimowski. My grandma was Rochel Nochimowski-- from the-- from the house, [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was my mother, in the back of this-- her father. In the middle, I was. And this is a cousin from Paris. This was, too, on the left, to the right, my mother's sister, one from United States--

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yente. And the other one is Nachamka. And in the middle stays my mother. In the back stays their son, Moishe Celevan. My brother, my brother, Motele, what he is in Israel. Then me. Yankele, und Itchzak.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was not far from our house, near the place where they made the [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you say [NON-ENGLISH]? Monument.

speaker: interviewee
text: Monument for Adam Mickiewicz. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: '37 I think. Yeah, they came from United States. This is a picture, not far from the house, probably in the back. That's me, Sonia, my Mother Pesia, and my baby brother, Yankele-- in Novogrudok. It has to be during the last years. I think '37, '38. This is, too, a picture when they came for United States. This must be about '37. Everything was in '37, 30-- '37, '36. That's Nachamke, my mother's sister, my mother in the middle, my other one Tante Yente, from New York. Next, my father in the back, and me. That's my two brothers. Both of them not alive. One is Itzchak and one is Zalman. Itzchak fell in Prussia, from the Germans. And Zalman, the second, fell in Palmach in Israel in '45. He-- Itzchak fell in Prussia, about a week before the war finished, in '44. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Litva. They were waiting for the plane to United States. They had ticket to go to United States. This is the backside from the kitchen. I'm sitting with my mother in the back, from the kitchen, in the window. Me and mother. I think too that's probably '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a picture of me. This was when they came, the Russian came in '49, yes? This probably was in '48, something. Because I am wearing already the red sign that I'm finishing the [NON-ENGLISH]. Did you get that date right? In 19 what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian came.

speaker: interviewer
text: In when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '49. No-- sorry, the Russian came in-- I forgot. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] '43? Oh, '39. I made a mistake. OK. Right? '39. So in '39, this was already, you see, or I have here on mine-- mine-- here on my coat, I have a red sign. This is a sign from the [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is in red, that I'm finishing in another half a year. This is it. And I was waiting for my boyfriends to come. And he didn't come so quick. So somebody went and made me the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture was taken maybe a week before the Russian came to the forest. The Russian came to the forest in '43. Right? This is probably in June, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: This Levine-- we had somebody, Levine, a partisan of ours. And he had a apparatus. He said, come on Zusia and Sonia. We will make a picture. You will remember me. And really this is the picture what he made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can I say to you? This is partisans from our otriad-- from the Bielski's otriad. They were always changing. They were staying in the-- waiting for the planes to come from Moscow, and to take the packages from Moscow, what they were sending us. They were sending us medicine, they were sending us whatever we needed. They mostly, they were sending medicine and the [NON-ENGLISH], the what-- how does it called, [NON-ENGLISH]? Bullets.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bullets, yes. And this is Abramovich on the far right, is Abramovich Yankel. And then I have Levine, and sitting near him is Yule Levine. And the rest, I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we went out from the forest, in '44. So probably this was in the beginning of '44. Because there really the people, the-- the planes were-- begin to come to us in the beginning of '44. From the left, to the right is Assael, that's the middle one. Assael, Tuvia, and Zusia, my husband. The three brothers. That's me and my husband in the forest about a week before we left the forest. And Yule Levine made it. This is the 53rd anniversary, the last anniversary for me and for my husband. And this is our sons with the families, with the grandchildren. We have my three sons, and the wives, and the grandchildren. Only one is missing. She was a baby. She's still not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. This is Matthew, and Danielle.

speaker: interviewer
text: From which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's from my son's.

speaker: interviewer
text: From which side of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left. From the left. The grandchildren from the left. So Matthew and Danielle, and then we have Becky and Ilan. And we have Rachel, David's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: The back that's the wives, and the boys. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to give us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my son, Zvi.

speaker: interviewer
text: On which side?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the left side. And he has a cousin there, who came from Israel. Then we have-- we have to a cousin from Israel, kibbutz Dan. Now we have Jay and his wife, and David and his wife. Yeah. And I, and they-- Rachel is sitting on my knee, in the left side. This is our anniversary-- the 53rd anniversary of mine, and my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the old pictures from Novogrudok?

speaker: interviewee
text: The old pictures I got from my brother in Israel. When we were home, still in Novogrudok, we were sending in pictures from the house. So when I came to Israel, he was showing me the pictures we send them before. That's what I have the couple of pictures. If not, we wouldn't have had the pictures, the old ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Mrs. Bielski, would you introduce your sons to us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from the right is my oldest son, Jay. And he's the first born. He was born in Israel, in-- in December 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then to your left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the left is David. He was born, too, in Israel. And he was born in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Jay-- Dr. Jay Bielski, would you like to say anything to, or about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: He has a big mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] In relation to the surviving, I was born in '46, the first couple of years in Israel. And they were still traumatized in those early years. And it took a long time for them to get acclimated to normal social endeavors. However, she always had the pride of a bull dog. And my father always, till his last day, was always a very prideful man, very proud of being Jewish. And I guess we-- I was in Israel in the army. My brother was in Israel in the army. Zvi was not here. And we always had the notion that we are warriors in a Jewish society, generally thought of as sheep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Mr. David Bielski?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's to say when you listen to people tell you stories about your mom, and your dad, and what they went through, and what they came from, and-- and how they survived and-- with so much strength? And not looking back, but actually going through-- going through life, emotionally and physically, through a time that I couldn't imagine myself, being thrown into a time like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- it kind of puts everything in perspective. When you think-- When I think that I did something bad, or did something wrong, or I'm going through a hard time, and I can't hold on, I want to have a nervous breakdown. I just need help. I want to go see a psychiatrist, or-- or anything. It is so minute compared to what they went through, that it just makes everything very, very small compared to going through a time like that, where the Jewish people were slaughtered and persecuted, and they were able to-- to rise above all that. And with the help of other people and through other people, really, made a strong, a good, and a very figurative family name for this family. You know, and it just kind-- just kind of puts everything into perspective, you know? Thank you very much.
